content.claim: Hungary's system combines early academic tracking with unrestricted free school choice and student commuting; towns with higher student commuting to chosen schools show stronger inter-school inequalities, suggesting that the selection mechanism in Hungary is not tracking alone but the interaction of tracking with choice—a bundled institutional context not present in the same form in Germany or comprehensive Nordic systems.
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: The causal direction (does choice amplify inequality caused by tracking, or do existing inequalities drive choice patterns?) is not definitively established.

Germany's federal system and recommendation-based assignment also involve choice variations, so the claim that the tracking-plus-choice bundle is unique to Hungary requires more precise comparative specification.
Isolating the effect of tracking age from the effect of free choice would require Hungarian-specific quasi-experimental evidence not cited in the notes.
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
